Q1. It was a bright sunny day when the 3 friends Ria, Sia and Tia went for a morning walk. Ria planned to give hand made masks to every one so that everybody is safe from germs. Sia helped her in making 9 masks and Tia helped her in making 7 masks. Ria thanked everyone for being helpful and working as a team. How many masks did they make altogether?

Q2. Tanya was the owner of a nursery. She loved natural beauty of the city. In order to save the environment, she thought of distributing plants among her customers, so that each one of them could plant and enhance the natural beauty of the city. On Monday she distributed 41 plants and on Wednesday she distributed 18 plants. How many total number of plants did she distribute?

Q3. One day Rina's teachers thought of making a big box holder with some spare stationery for a village school. She told Rina to bring as many ice cream sticks as she could arrange. The next day Rina brought 92 ice cream sticks to the class. Her teacher already had 6 ice cream sticks. How many total ice cream sticks did they have to make the box?

___

Observe the given table and answer the following questions.

Q1. The teacher asks Ron to find out the favourite snack of his friends. Ron collects the data and records it in the table shown below. Observe the given data and answer the questions:

Snack Burger Fries Brownie
Number of friends83458
A. How many friends like ice-cream?
B. How many friends like burger?
c. How many friends like pizza?
D. Which snack is liked by 5 of his friends?
E. Which two snacks add up to 7?
F. How many friends like brownie and burger?

Q2. Sam sells many items in his shop. The table below shows the number of items sold different days of the week. Observe the given data and answer the questions.

Monday 🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔

Tuesday 🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔

Wednesday 🏠🏠🏠

Thursday 🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔

Friday 🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔

Saturday 🏠🏠🏠🏠

A. How many clocks were sold on Friday?

B. Which item was sold on Saturday?

C. On which day maximum number of items were sold?

D. On which day the least number of items were sold?

parsing_res_list.block_content: Grade: 1Subject: MathsTopic: High Order Cognitive Abilities
On a Sunday morning, you saw your mother organise your cupboard. What is the first thing you should do? You should help your mother organise your cupboard. Right?

First, you take out everything from the cupboard and spread it neatly on the floor or on the bed. You look carefully at each item. You group similar things together — all the books in one place, all the clothes in another, all the toys in another and all the socks together. Then, you count the items in each group. Maybe you find 14 books, 9 toys, 15 clothes and 5 pairs of socks. (Just an example). You compare the groups and write/stick the picture which group has more, which has less and which has the same number.

Next, you decide where each group should go. You keep big and heavy items on the lower shelves and light and small items on the upper shelves. You arrange your school books in ascending order and your clothes in the descending order of size from the biggest to the smallest. Try noticing the place value of the number of books too.

Now it is time to fix your socks. You pair them in groups of two and find out if the total number of socks is even or odd. If one sock is left without a pair, you know the number is odd. If all socks are paired, the number is even.

Then you move to your toys. You arrange them on the shelf and make number bonds of 10. For example, if you keep 6 toys in one box, how many more are needed to make 10? You add some more toys and see:  $ 6 + 4 = 10 $,  $ 7 + 3 = 10 $,  $ 5 + 5 = 10 $. You are using addition without even realising it!
Finally, you make up a small story about how you spent Sunday morning arranging your cupboard with the help of your mother!
Do you feel proud of yourself as you used sorting, comparing, counting, addition, number bonds, place value and ordering to make your cupboard beautiful.

markdown.text:
Grade: 1Subject: MathsTopic: High Order Cognitive Abilities
Date: ___ On a Sunday morning, you saw your mother organise your cupboard. What is the first thing you should do? You should help your mother organise your cupboard. Right? First, you take out everything from the cupboard and spread it neatly on the floor or on the bed. You look carefully at each item. You group similar things together — all the books in one place, all the clothes in another, all the toys in another and all the socks together. Then, you count the items in each group. Maybe you find 14 books, 9 toys, 15 clothes and 5 pairs of socks. (Just an example). You compare the groups and write/stick the picture which group has more, which has less and which has the same number. Next, you decide where each group should go. You keep big and heavy items on the lower shelves and light and small items on the upper shelves. You arrange your school books in ascending order and your clothes in the descending order of size from the biggest to the smallest. Try noticing the place value of the number of books too. Now it is time to fix your socks. You pair them in groups of two and find out if the total number of socks is even or odd. If one sock is left without a pair, you know the number is odd. If all socks are paired, the number is even. Then you move to your toys. You arrange them on the shelf and make number bonds of 10. For example, if you keep 6 toys in one box, how many more are needed to make 10? You add some more toys and see: $ 6 + 4 = 10 $, $ 7 + 3 = 10 $, $ 5 + 5 = 10 $. You are using addition without even realising it! Finally, you make up a small story about how you spent Sunday morning arranging your cupboard with the help of your mother! Do you feel proud of yourself as you used sorting, comparing, counting, addition, number bonds, place value and ordering to make your cupboard beautiful.
